coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots from pyrolytic condensation of aspartic acid and glycerol for ratiometric fluorosensing of lactic acid in cancer cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Asif, Seikh
Morgan, David
Sen, Kamalika
coredata.dc:description: In this study, strongly luminescent Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) of high quantum yield (QY ≈ 46 %) were synthesized by pyrolysis of aspartic acid in glycerol. Here we report the use of GQDs as a fluorescence ratiometric probe towards lactic acid (LA) in both serum and MCF-7 cell lysate medium. A remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) was found in serum medium (0.294 μM) and in MCF-7 cell extract (6.917 μM). Isothermal titration calorimetric studies yielded highly negative (−80.0 kcal mol−1) ΔH value in serum medium which suggested exothermic interactions between GQDs and lactic acid. The sensing capacity of the GQDs was further validated in presence of various interfering agents such as ascorbic acid, citric acid, nitrate, phosphate, glucose, cholesterol and uric acid in serum medium. Monitoring the amounts of LA in cancer cells is crucial as LA plays a significant role in the proliferation of cells. We therefore evaluated the performance of our ratiometric probe in measuring the intracellular LA concentration in MCF-7 cell lysate. The calibration curve of LA in MCF-7 cell lysate generated alongside in the concentration range 4.180–42.37 μM helped in accurately identifying the concentration of LA in cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Lactic acid
MCF-7
Cancer biomarker
Fluorescence
Ratiometric probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of lactic acid by GQDs
Fluorescence property and fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs
Fluorescence sensing study of LA by GQDs
Studies on mechanism of interaction of LA with GQDs
Comparison of the analytical performance of the present LA sensing with some of the reported procedures

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: SA acknowledges the award and funding of DST-INSPIRE fellowship (No. DST/INSPIRE Fellowship/2020/IF200392). We are thankful to Ms. Urmila Goswami, Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Kolkata, India for obtaining the TEM images. We are also grateful to RUSA facilities for funding the spectrofluorometer. We also thank DST FIST (SR/FST/CS-II/2017/27(C) dated 29.09.2018 for funding the isothermal calorimetry instrument. We also acknowledge CAS-V (UGC) (540/3/CASV/2015) (SAP\u2013I) for the funding of UV\u2013Visible spectrophotometer. We express our sincere thanks to Prof. Kajal Krishna Rajak, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, India for obtaining FTIR data. We are grateful to Dr. Ujjal Das, UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8, Bidhannagar, Kolkata, India for supplying us with MCF-7 cells.

SA acknowledges the award and funding of DST-INSPIRE fellowship (No. DST/INSPIRE Fellowship/2020/IF200392 ). We are thankful to Ms. Urmila Goswami, Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Kolkata, India for obtaining the TEM images. We are also grateful to RUSA facilities for funding the spectrofluorometer. We also thank DST FIST ( SR/FST/CS-II/2017/27(C ) dated 29.09.2018 for funding the isothermal calorimetry instrument. We also acknowledge CAS-V (UGC) ( 540/3/CASV/2015 ) (SAP I) for the funding of UV\u2013Visible spectrophotometer. We express our sincere thanks to Prof. Kajal Krishna Rajak, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, India for obtaining FTIR data. We are grateful to Dr. Ujjal Das, UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8, Bidhannagar, Kolkata, India for supplying us with MCF-7 cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (b) Lattice fringes (c) SAED pattern (d) Histogram of GQDs.
(A) C 1 s (B) N 1 s and (C) O 1 s XPS spectra of the synthesized GQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs with increasing concentration of lactic acid in serum prepared in RL medium. The spike of LA was increased in the concentration range 5.813–26.59 μM (λex = 307 nm, λem = 344 nm & 425 nm) (b) The calibration curve for LA in serum medium in the range 5.813–26.59 μM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs with increasing concentration of lactic acid in MCF-7 cell lysate prepared in RL medium. The spike of LA was increased in the concentration range 4.180–42.37 μM (λex = 307 nm, λem = 344 nm & 425 nm) (b) The calibration curve for LA in MCF-7 cell lysate medium in the range 4.180–42.37 μM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of increasing aliquots of MCF-7 cancer cell lysate in response to our sensor system prepared with GQDs in RL medium (b) The corresponding plot showing increase in intensity ratio (I344/I425) with increase in concentration of MCF-7 cancer cell lysate.
FTIR spectra of GQDs (green), GQDs treated with LA (blue).
TEM image of GQDs (a) without LA (b) with LA.
ITC results of GQDs-lactic acid interaction.
Schematic representation of synthesis of GQDs
Peaks of FTIR spectrum showing different functionalities.

row.entry: out-of-plane CH bending, CN stretching from pyridinic N, graphitic N
-OH in plane bending vibration, CH stretch
N − H and O − H stretching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Survey spectrum derived elemental analysis (atomic %) result of the synthesized GQDs.
Specific values of parameters for calculating QY of the GQDs.
Time-resolved fluorescence decay parameters of GQDs.
Various analytical parameters for the fluorometric detection of LA in serum medium by GQDs.
Zeta potential values of GQDs in different conditions.
Summary of affinity measurements of the interaction between GQDs and LA.
A comparative study of LA sensing by different reported methods with the proposed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes:- PB: phosphate buffer, PBS: phosphate-buffered saline, AuE: gold electrode, rGO: reduced graphene oxide, AgNPs: silver nanoparticles, MIP: molecularly imprinted polymer, NiF: nickel foam, HS-NiS: hollow sphere structured nickel sulfide, L-DOPA: L-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine, bCDs: blue emission carbon dots, rQDs: red emission quantum dots PCF: photonic crystal fiber.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots from pyrolytic condensation of aspartic acid and glycerol for ratiometric fluorosensing of lactic acid in cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calcutta, 92 APC Road, Kolkata 700009, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Calcutta, 92 APC Road, Kolkata 700009

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cardiff Catalysis Institute, School of Chemistry, Cardiff University, Park Place, Cardiff CF10 3AT, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cardiff Catalysis Institute, School of Chemistry, Cardiff University, Park Place, Cardiff, CF10 3AT

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, strongly luminescent Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) of high quantum yield (QY ≈ 46 %) were synthesized by pyrolysis of aspartic acid in glycerol. Here we report the use of GQDs as a fluorescence ratiometric probe towards lactic acid (LA) in both serum and MCF-7 cell lysate medium. A remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) was found in serum medium (0.294 μM) and in MCF-7 cell extract (6.917 μM). Isothermal titration calorimetric studies yielded highly negative (−80.0 kcal mol−1) ΔH value in serum medium which suggested exothermic interactions between GQDs and lactic acid. The sensing capacity of the GQDs was further validated in presence of various interfering agents such as ascorbic acid, citric acid, nitrate, phosphate, glucose, cholesterol and uric acid in serum medium. Monitoring the amounts of LA in cancer cells is crucial as LA plays a significant role in the proliferation of cells. We therefore evaluated the performance of our ratiometric probe in measuring the intracellular LA concentration in MCF-7 cell lysate. The calibration curve of LA in MCF-7 cell lysate generated alongside in the concentration range 4.180–42.37 μM helped in accurately identifying the concentration of LA in cancer cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is well documented for its fatality, especially in its advanced stages [1]. However, malignancies that are diagnosed early have a good survival rate [2]. Consequently, early detection is crucial for increasing survival percentage of cancer patients [3]. The detection of cancer cells and circulating biomarkers is the primary method for early cancer diagnosis [4]. The precise identification of cancer cells is a major challenge since these are intermingled with leukocytes and erythrocytes in peripheral blood and are present at such low concentrations in the early stages [5]. The association between biomarkers and cancer continues to be incrementally simplified as research has progressed [6]. Detecting biomarkers in body fluids (such blood, urine, and saliva) as well as in cell extracts can offer a secure and practical way to identify cancer early on [7].

When oxygen supply to the tissues is drastically constrained, lactic acid (LA) is generated as a by-product. Glucose which is the main energy source in our body is metabolized to pyruvate through glycolysis. The oxygen content of the tissue then determines the fate of pyruvate. Pyruvate enters mitochondria under aerobic conditions, where it undergoes the pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) reaction to produce acetyl coenzyme A (acetyl-CoA). Acetyl-CoA then enters the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle to undergo metabolism into CO2, H2O, and energy metabolites [8].

Glycolysis coupled with oxidative phosphorylation produces up to 38 molecules of ATP per molecule of glucose. However, in anaerobic environment, the lactate dehydrogenase enzyme (LDH) reduces pyruvate to lactate, which is then released into the bloodstream. This excess build-up of lactate in the bloodstream leads to lactic acidosis. Lactic acidosis is classified into two categories: Type A and Type B. Type A arises due to hypoxic conditions in the body. Type B arises in persons suffering from metabolic disorders such as diabetes mellitus, renal or liver disease or severe infections [9]. In cancer cells, the rate of consumption of glucose rises sharply and even in presence of abundant O2 through glycolysis, lactic acid is produced instead of ATP. This is known as Warburg effect [10].

In healthy people, LA levels in blood can range from 0.5 to 1 mM. More than 4 mM is observed in case of certain disorders, which also include cancer (during the metastatic spread of malignant cells in stages II or III) [11]. As a result, swift and exact identification of the amount of LA can aid in the early diagnosis of cancer as well as monitoring the progression and severity of the disease. There are two basic approaches of lactic acid detection: enzymatic and non-enzymatic. Enzymatic-based biosensors, typically involving the immobilization of lactate oxidase [12] or LDH enzyme [13], encounter several limitations related to enzyme utilization. Commonly, enzymes struggle to withstand extreme pH and temperature conditions; they are designed for single use, can easily leak from the sensing support, and tend to be costly. Consequently, the importance of non-enzymatic approaches for the detection of LA has emerged. The fundamental analytical methods for lactic acid detection include electrochemical analysis [14], chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry [15], colorimetry [16], micro assay [17], and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fluorescence spectroscopy is a viable choice among these because of its simplicity of use, high sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, quick response time, and low background noise.

The practical applications of traditional organic small molecule and semiconductor quantum dot fluorescent probes have been severely restricted in recent years due to their high toxicity, low biocompatibility, and low water solubility [18]. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), a new class of nanomaterials, are novel zero-dimensional graphitic nanomaterials with quantum constraint, size effect, and edge effect that have great potential for use in biological sensing due to their low toxicity, excellent water dispersibility, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, photostability, ease of synthesis, and exceptional photoluminescence properties [19].

This work involves the synthesis, characterization, and investigation of GQDs as a fluorophore for the sensitive measurement of lactic acid in both human serum and MCF-7 (breast cancer cell line) cell lysate medium. In the current work, aspartic acid was pyrolyzed in glycerol medium to prepare GQDs. A ratiometric fluorescence probe towards lactic acid has been developed. The development of ratiometric fluorescence instead of monochromatic fluorescence improves the stability and accuracy of the detection method. The two detection signals for the ratiometric fluorescence probe can increase the sensitivity and accuracy of the fluorescent probe while preventing external variables from affecting the results of the experiment [20]. The developed probe was applied to lactic acid detection in human serum and cancer cell lysate medium with satisfactory results, indicating that the probe may have enormous potential in clinical practice.

Aspartic acid, lactic acid, ascorbic acid and cholesterol were procured from LOBA Chemie, India. Glycerol (anhydrous), citric acid, sodium nitrate, di‑sodium hydrogen phosphate dihydrate, dextrose (anhydrous) each were purchased from Merck Pvt. Ltd., India. Ringer-Lactate solution (RL) (each 100 mL aqueous solution contains sodium lactate (0.32 % w/v), sodium chloride (0.60 g), potassium chloride (0.04 g), calcium chloride (0.04 g)) was obtained from local pharmacy. Dialysis tubing cellulose membrane (molecular weight cut-off ∼14,000 kDa) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Throughout the experiment, triple distilled water was used.

A Horiba fluoromax plus spectrofluorometer was used to perform fluorescence spectroscopic measurements. The excited state fluorescence lifetime measurement was carried out on Horiba Jobin Yvon Deltaflex NL-C2 using a picosecond laser diode of 314 nm. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic data was recorded in the range of 3500 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 using Perkin-Elmer FTIR spectrometer (spectrum two) with the samples following the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique. A JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) with 200 kV electron source was used to get the TEM images and selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern of the synthesized GQDs. A Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument was used for zeta potential (ζ) measurement. A Malvern MicroCal PEAQ-ITC instrument was used to perform isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) experiment using the MicroCal PEAQ-ITC Analysis Software. A Kratos Axis Ultra DLD system was used to collect XPS spectra using monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source operating at 120 W (10 mA × 12 kV). Data was collected with pass energies of 160 eV for survey spectra, and 40 eV for the high-resolution scans with step sizes of 1 eV and 0.1 eV respectively. Samples were mounted by pressing in to a recess made by a copper washer mounted using double sided adhesive tape (Scotch 665) mounted on to a clean glass microscope slide which was taped to a standard Kratos sample bar. The system was operated in the Hybrid mode, using a combination of magnetic immersion and electrostatic lenses and acquired over an area approximately 300 × 700 μm2. A magnetically confined charge compensation system was used to minimize charging of the sample surface, and all spectra were taken with a 90° take off angle. A base pressure of ca. 5 × 10−9 Torr was maintained during collection of the spectra. Data was analyzed using CasaXPS (v2.3.26) [21] after subtraction of a Tougaard polymer background and using modified Wagner sensitivity factors as supplied by the manufacturer. For charge calibration, the lowest C(1 s) signal was set to an energy of 284.5 eV, typical of graphitic like carbon.

The GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing aspartic acid and glycerol. 0.5 g of aspartic acid and 10 mL glycerol were taken into a 100 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle with constant stirring on a magnetic stirrer (250 rpm) and then allowed to cool to room temperature. Subsequently, the colour of the liquid changed from colourless to pale yellow, and then orange in 40 min, implying the formation of GQDs. The orange product thus obtained was subjected to dialysis for 24 h using a dialysis membrane. Then the GQDs processed by dialysis were stored at room temperature as stock solution for further characterization and use. The possible reactions involved during the process are shown in the schematic diagram (Scheme 1 ).

The synthesized GQDs were characterized using a variety of analytical tools in an attempt to ascertain their intrinsic characteristics. The GQDs stock solution was drop-cast onto a carbon coated copper grid. After drying under an IR lamp, the grid was used to record TEM micrographs, lattice fringes, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns in order to examine their structural morphologies, including size, shape, and crystallinity. To ascertain the effective charge generated on the surface of GQDs, zeta potential (ζ) measurement was performed. The measurement provides insight into how stable the GQDs are in fluid suspension. A drop of the GQDs stock solution was placed on the ATR crystal, and the FTIR spectra were recorded in order to tap the surface functionalities using FTIR spectroscopy. XPS analysis was used to investigate the elemental composition and bonding on the surface of the synthesized GQDs.

The fluorescence QY of the GQDs in water was calculated using the following equation (eq. 1) [22] at an excitation wavelength of 307 nm, using Rhodamine B in water as a reference dye.
(1)
QY GQDs
=
QY R
A R
/
A GQDs
F GQDs
/
F R
n GQDs
/
n R
2
where QY is fluorescence quantum yield, F is the observed integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance of the solution at the excitation wavelength, n is the refractive index of the solvent, while subscripts ‘R' and ‘GQDs' refer to the reference used (Rhodamine B, QY = 0.31 in water) and the sample respectively.

By tracking the variation in fluorescence intensity with irradiation time under UV light, the photostability of the GQDs was examined. The sample was constantly exposed to UV light (365 nm) for six hours and fluorescence intensity was measured intermittently in order to investigate the photostability of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of lactic acid by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing ability of the synthesized GQDs was investigated using intrinsic fluorescence property of the GQDs at 344 nm and 425 nm. The excitation wavelength was selected at 307 nm. 100 μL of human serum was diluted to 1 mL using 0.4 mL of RL and 0.5 mL of 500 μM LA. Different known aliquots of this mixture was added to sensor system consisting of 0.2 mL of GQDs stock and 1.27 mL RL with proper mixing in a quartz cuvette with an optical path length of 1 cm. The changes in fluorescence intensity of the GQDs with successive addition were instantly recorded. A previous calibration was made by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio I344/I425 vs. the concentration of LA (where, I344 and I425 are the fluorescence intensities of GQDs at 344 nm and 425 nm respectively). The selectivity of the developed sensor refers to its capability to detect LA in the presence of certain interfering substances that may coexist with LA in biological fluid. The interference investigation was conducted by studying the changes in fluorescence intensity that occurred in the presence of several foreign species in excess, such as citric acid, ascorbic acid, uric acid, glucose, cholesterol, nitrate, and phosphate. Keeping the concentration of each of these interferants 1 mM in the serum-LA mixture, the sensing assays in presence of these interfering biomolecules were carried out.
Similarly, a sensing assay for LA was performed in cell lysate medium. The preparation of the cell lysate is discussed in the following section below.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of LA were computed using eqs. (2 and 3) based on the slope (S) and standard deviation (σ) of the calibration curve of the responses.
(2)
LOD = 3.3
σ / S
(3)
LOQ = 10
σ / S

MCF-7 (human breast cancer cell line) was purchased from the cell repository of National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS), Pune. They were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS), 100 μg/mL streptomycin and 100 units/mL penicillin. They were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified environment with 5 % CO2.

The culture medium was removed from the culture flask containing the confluent layer of MCF-7 cells. Then the flask was washed with ice-cold phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer (pH 7.4). After that trypsin-EDTA solution was added in the flask and incubated in a 37 °C CO2 incubator for 1–2 min. After complete detachment, the cells were suspended in culture medium in a falcon and centrifuged to pellet down the cells. The cell pellet was then suspended in ice-cold Radio Immunoprecipitation Assay (RIPA) (1 % Triton X-100, 50 mM Tris HCl (pH 7.4), 150 mM NaCl, 0.1 % sodium deoxycholate, 1 mM EDTA, and 0.1 % sodium dodecyl sulfate [SDS]) lysis buffer to aid in cell lysis. The cell lysate was incubated on ice for approximately 30 min. The cell lysate was clarified by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C. After centrifugation, the clear supernatant was collected and stored at −20 °C for future experiments.

The TEM image of the GQDs in Fig. 1a, shows nearly spherical shape with a relatively uniform size with diameter ranging from 2 to 3 nm. Fig. 1b shows the HRTEM image of the GQDs with a clear lattice fine structure, indicating its high crystallinity.

Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of GQDs calculated from ImageJ software is about 0.28 nm, indicating the characteristic structure of the graphitic carbon [23]. Bright concentric rings that appear in the SAED pattern (Fig. 1c) of the GQDs are an indication that these nanoparticles are polycrystalline in nature. Narrow particle size distribution with size 2–3 nm could be seen from particle size histogram (Fig. 1d).

The zeta potential is a critical parameter for assessing the surface charge of a nanoparticle and predicting its long-term stability in solution. The magnitude of the zeta potential reflects the colloidal system's potential stability. In order to prevent aggregation, particles in suspension that have a high negative or positive zeta potential tend to repel one another. The surface of the synthesized GQDs became negatively charged, as indicated by the zeta potential value of −22.3 mV, which may be due to the hydroxyl functional groups present at their surface (further confirmed from FTIR spectroscopy). Such a value of zeta potential suggests that the GQDs are reasonably stable in the solution, in accordance with the parameters used to categorize the nanoparticle dispersions [24,25].

FTIR spectroscopy was used to analyze the chemical structure of the synthesized GQDs. Fig. 2 depicts the FTIR spectrum of GQDs, and Table 1 lists the potential functionalities found in GQDs. The broad bands at around 3000–3400 cm−1 correspond to N − H and O − H stretching vibrations [26]. The peaks at 1044 cm−1 [27] and 1220 cm−1 [28] correspond to epoxy (C-O-C) group. The peaks at 1412 cm−1 and 1113 cm−1 can be attributed to -OH in plane bending vibration of carboxylic acid or CH bending vibration in graphitic systems [29,30] and CO stretching [31] respectively. In addition, the bands at 2945 cm−1 and 2886 cm−1 arise due to stretching vibration of CH bond [32]. Importantly, the FTIR peak at 1648 cm−1 points towards the characteristic aromatic CC ring stretching thus confirming the presence of graphitic lattice in GQDs [33]. Typically, the vibrations under 1000 cm−1 represent out-of-plane CH bending of the aromatic system [34]. The strong peak at 773 cm−1, may be due to the CN bond from pyridinic N, graphitic N, and/or pyrrolic N present in the GQDs [35]. Specifically, it indicates the presence of nitrogen atoms incorporated into the graphene structure, likely as part of pyridinic, graphitic, or pyrrolic nitrogen functionalities. These nitrogen atoms can influence the electronic properties and photoluminescence of GQDs. The presence of pyridinic nitrogen has also been confirmed from the XPS study. The high hydrophilicity of GQDs is due to the presence of oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl).
XPS analysis was carried out to look into the chemical structure of the GQDs in more detail. The atomic percentage of the elemental composition of the GQDs is displayed in Table 2 .

The C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3A) of GQDs shows major peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.54 eV, 286.3 eV, 287.9 eV and 288.9 eV which can be attributed to C-C/C-H [36], CN [37], CO [37], and CO [37,38] bonding respectively. Additionally, peaks at 292.7 eV and 295.6 eV indicate some potassium, which is superimposed on any weak structure due to the shake up π-π* satellite peak, a distinctive feature of aromatic rings and presence of π-π bond respectively in the GQDs [39]. There is one peak positioned at 400.0 eV in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3B) indicating the presence of CN or NHx functions, can also refer to the presence of pyridinic nitrogen (N-(C)3) in the GQDs [36]. The peak at 532.2 eV [38] in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3C) correspond to CO group. Also, another peak at 533.34 eV [38] associated with the presence of CO bonding appears in the O 1 s spectrum.

Based on the XPS data, it can be inferred that although there are some S and Si present, these signals may also consist of SiO and SO bonds (which are present as impurities) and would be found at binding energies around those noted for the CO and CO functions. The carbon network has undergone significant modification, leading to the conclusion that the surface of GQDs has a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, which gives the GQDs their high aqueous solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence property and fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized GQDs were found to exhibit intense fluorescence emission upon excitation at 307 nm. From literature the influence of the surface functional groups on the fluorescence of GQDs is well established. The presence of these surface functional groups enables electronic transitions such as π* → n and σ* → n transitions between the antibonding and the bonding molecular orbitals, resulting in fluorescence [40]. Also, upon introduction of functional groups, GQDs consist of both sp2 and sp3 carbons bonded with atoms having higher electron densities and result in fluorescence [41]. As shown in the reaction scheme (Scheme 1), the presence of oxygen functional groups (hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl) contributes to fluorescence through the electronic transitions as described above. For the GQDs, the excitation is at 307 nm with two emission peaks at 344 and 425 nm. Two emission peaks for one excitation can occur when a molecule has multiple electronic states that can be excited and then decay to different lower energy states. These transitions can result in emission at different wavelengths, leading to multiple peaks in the emission spectrum [42]. Table 3 enlists the parameters used for calculating the QY of the GQDs which has a considerably high value (46 %).

Fluorescence lifetime of GQDs was measured using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique using a 314 nm laser diode source, and the decay was observed at the emission maxima (425 nm). The average fluorescence lifetime is calculated from the eq. (4).
(4)
τ
avg .
=
Σ i
α i
τ i
2
/
Σ i
α i
τ i
where α
i represents amplitude, τ
i represents decay time and τ
avg. represents average fluorescence lifetime. As evident from the decay plot (Fig. S1) and the corresponding decay parameters (Table 4 ), GQDs displayed a bi-exponential fit with favorable χ2 value. The fluorescence lifetime of the GQDs is found to be 6.70 ns.
From Fig. S2 it can be seen that the GQDs retained their fluorescence intensity up to 86 % of the initial value even after 6 h of UV exposure and no significant photobleaching was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing study of LA by GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the viability of our GQDs to be used as a fluorescence-based sensing platform for lactic acid, we evaluated how the fluorescence intensities of our GQDs changed as the concentration of LA increased (Fig. 4a). With gradual increase in concentration of LA, an increase in fluorescence intensity at 344 nm with concomitant decrease in fluorescence intensity at 425 nm was found. In the range of 5.813–26.59 μM, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I344/I425) and the concentration of LA. Fig. 4b exhibited that the calibration curve fits a regression equation of I344/I425 = 0.020 [LA] +0.213 with R2 = 0.999 (where [LA] is the concentration of LA). The limit of detection based on the equation LOD = 3.3 (σ/S) (where σ is the standard deviation and S is the slope of the calibration curve of the responses) was 0.294 μM which is comparable to most other existing methods of LA detection. The result indicates that the GQDs can be used as a ratiometric probe for monitoring LA concentration in correlation with the changes in fluorescence intensity ratio. To check for a control (without external addition of LA) a similar experiment was also performed, where ratiometric fluorescence change was not observed at all (Fig. S3).

One metabolic characteristic of cancer is high rate of glycolysis. Aerobic glycolysis, also known as the Warburg effect, provides a proliferative advantage by rerouting glucose from energy generation to biosynthetic pathways. The glycolytic process is linked to increased lactic acid output in cancer cells [43]. Therefore, to evaluate and compare the sensing in practical situations, the MCF-7 cell lysate was spiked with a series of various concentrations of LA and the plot of I344/I425 versus the concentration of LA was drawn. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs gradually increased at 344 nm with concurrent decrease of the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm over the concentration range 4.180–42.37 μM (Fig. 5a). The plot in Fig. 5b shows that there is a good linear relationship between I344/I425 and LA concentration with a limit of detection 6.917 μM. The linear regression equation was I344/I425 = 0.024 [LA] + 0.161, with a coefficient of determination R2 = 0.981. Given the importance of lactic acid in proliferating cell function, it is of utter importance to monitor the levels of LA in cancer cells. In this context, we evaluated the performance of our ratiometric probe in measuring the intracellular lactic acid concentration in MCF-7 cell lysate. The calibration curve of LA in MCF-7 cell lysate generated alongside helps in accurately identifying the concentration of LA in cancer cells. For the aforementioned purpose, known aliquots (containing unknown LA concentration) of cancer cell lysate were added gradually to our sensor system. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, in response to our sensor system with gradual increase in cancer cell lysate, the I344/I425 was found to increase systematically with increasing LA concentration (as obtained from the calibration plot, Fig. 5b).

With increasing aliquots of cell lysate, the I344/I425 ratio increased in the same fashion generating the same linear equation I344/I425 = 0.024 [LA] + 0.161, thereby affirming that our ratiometric fluorescent sensor can act as an effective tool for monitoring lactic acid in biological samples such as in vitro cell systems and can be used as a cancer sensing unit.

Fluorometric experiments were performed to determine the interaction between GQDs and LA at an excitation wavelength of 307 nm. It was observed that with increase in concentration of LA, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs decreased at 425 nm with simultaneous increase in fluorescence intensity at 344 nm. To further validate the selectivity of our proposed LA sensor we have carried out the sensing study in presence of various competing species. The level of LA in blood rises under hypoxic conditions [9]. Repetitive cycles of hypoxia can lead to hypoxic oxidative stress and accumulation of pyruvate through breakdown of glucose [44]. This accumulated pyruvate is converted to lactate by LDH. Due to lack of ATP in hypoxemic state, the utilization of LA in Cori cycle (in liver) through gluconeogenesis is inhibited [45]. This condition can lead to overproduction of uric acid (UA) due to accumulation of pyruvate and release of purine intermediates. Therefore, hypoxia can induce both high levels of UA [46] and LA [47]. Again, increased serum cholesterol concentration can lead to atherosclerosis reducing blood flow to heart thereby causing dearth of oxygen delivery and increasing the levels of lactic acid [9]. To reduce cardiovascular risk which is also a contributing factor to increased levels of LA, patients take isosorbide mononitrate. Now, prolonged use of this medication can cause build-up of nitrate in body. Again, during intense physical activity, anaerobic metabolism leads to lactic acidosis and increased levels of inorganic phosphate due to breakdown of phosphocreatine [48]. Patients suffering from diabetes mellitus are found to have high lactic levels in their blood, a condition known as lactic acidosis [9]. Also, ascorbic acid is known to stimulate lactate transport in body [49] and citric acid is a part of our body energy cycle (Krebs cycle). In presence of these interferants also, ratiometry is observed towards LA sensing which has been illustrated in Fig. S4. The LOD values obtained in presence of these interfering biomolecules is much less as compared to an otherwise healthy condition. Table 5 shows different analytical parameters associated with LA sensing by the GQDs along with their R2 values in serum medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on mechanism of interaction of LA with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The modes of interactions of LA with GQDs were studied through different analytical techniques which helped to find the structural and functional changes in GQDs after interactions.

To investigate the mode of interaction of lactic acid with GQDs, zeta potential measurement was carried out (Table 6 ). Here, we can see that the GQDs are negatively charged. However, when these GQDs are introduced in RL medium their surface becomes positively charged. This facilitates lactic acid which generally dissociates to lactate ions to bind with the surface of GQDs.

FTIR spectra were analyzed to get more insight into the interaction study of GQDs with LA. A noticeable change occurs when LA is introduced to GQDs, the peak corresponding to CC stretching (1648 cm−1) is totally diminished, which denotes the loss of aromaticity. This loss in aromaticity has also been confirmed from XPS data where the satellite peaks appearing due to the presence of aromatic structure of GQDs have completely disappeared on interaction with LA (Fig. S5). Also, the peak appearing at 1044 cm−1 due to presence of epoxy group in GQDs shows a significant increase of intensity on interaction with LA, appearing at 1048 cm−1. Besides three new peaks appear at 2349 cm−1, 2194 cm−1 and 1988 cm−1 on interaction with LA which point out to significant modification on the surface of GQDs on interaction with LA. The broad band in the 3000–3400 cm−1 region due to N − H and O − H stretching vibrations in the FTIR spectrum of GQDs decrease in intensity on interaction with LA implying cleavage of NH and OH bonds. The stacked FTIR spectra (Fig. 7 ) below show the interaction of GQDs with LA.

With regard to their excellent water solubility, GQDs tend to agglomerate as can be seen from the TEM image (Fig. 8a). Now, in water medium LA furnishes lactate ions. These lactate ions on interaction with the negatively charged GQDs cause them to repel each other thereby preventing aggregation and thus discrete particles of slightly smaller dimensions are observed on interaction of LA with GQDs as can be seen from the TEM image (Fig. 8b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the analytical performance of the present LA sensing with some of the reported procedures
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy and sensitivity of LA detection using GQDs as a ratiometric probe can be compared with the reports available in literature from Table 8 . It can be evidently seen that the detection limit of our proposed sensor using fluorescence spectroscopy is significantly better than other constructed sensors. In contemporary times, few works are reported in literature for the construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensors for lactic acid detection and those which are available mainly focus on quantifying LA based on enzyme catalyzed reactions which involve cumbersome sample preparations. Now, enzymes are single-use and expensive, parameters like temperature and pH play a huge role in the stability of enzymes. This paves the way for development of non-enzymatic approaches. Non-enzymatic approaches using detection methods like electrochemical, colorimetry involve extremely laborious sensor construction and complex assays unlike our sensor system. Additionally, sensing in actual biological fluid medium (serum/cancer cell extract) has not been dealt with in these reported methods.

The results of our sensor demonstrated the reliability of the ratiometric measurements during the quantification of intracellular lactic acid in MCF-7 cell lysate medium highlighting the robustness of our sensor compared with previously reported sensor systems.

In this novel study, we have developed a ratiometric probe based on GQDs with a high QY of 46 % for the determination of an important metabolism-based cancer biomarker LA in both serum and MCF-7 cell lysate medium. The detection limit was as low as 0.294 μM in serum medium. Furthermore, the selectivity of our sensor was validated in presence of various interfering biomolecules viz. glucose, cholesterol, ascorbic acid, citric acid, uric acid, nitrate and phosphate in serum medium. The LOD values obtained in presence of these interfering biomolecules is much less as compared to an otherwise healthy condition. The biosensor leveraged its LA-sensitivity in measuring the unknown intracellular lactic acid concentration in MCF-7 cell lysate using a calibration curve of LA in the same medium generated alongside. These findings highlighted the underlying versatility, robustness and accuracy of the proposed platform, providing a valuable tool for detecting LA in various biological media.

Seikh Asif: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. David Morgan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kamalika Sen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Kamalika Sen reports financial support was provided by Department of Science and Technology. Kamalika Sen reports a relationship with University of Calcutta University College of Science Technology and Agriculture that includes: employment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SA acknowledges the award and funding of DST-INSPIRE fellowship (No. DST/INSPIRE Fellowship/2020/IF200392). We are thankful to Ms. Urmila Goswami, Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Kolkata, India for obtaining the TEM images. We are also grateful to RUSA facilities for funding the spectrofluorometer. We also thank DST FIST (SR/FST/CS-II/2017/27(C) dated 29.09.2018 for funding the isothermal calorimetry instrument. We also acknowledge CAS-V (UGC) (540/3/CASV/2015) (SAPI) for the funding of UV–Visible spectrophotometer. We express our sincere thanks to Prof. Kajal Krishna Rajak, Jadavpur University, Kolkata, India for obtaining FTIR data. We are grateful to Dr. Ujjal Das, UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8, Bidhannagar, Kolkata, India for supplying us with MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.112547.
